name: CHEMISTRY (Theory)
question_paper_html:
Annual Exam - 2022

Code: 56/3/1 (set-1)

  • Please check that this question paper contains 12 printed pages.
  • Q.P. Code number given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  • Please check that this question paper contains 12 questions.
  • Please write down the Serial Number of the question in the answer-book before attempting it.
  • 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the candidates will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.

CHEMISTRY (Theory)

Time allowed:2 hours
Maximum Marks:35

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This question paper contains 12 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper is divided into three Sections - Section A, B and C.
  3. Section - A Q. Nos. 1 to 3 are very short answer type questions carrying 2 marks each.
  4. Section - B Q. Nos. 4 to 11 are short answer type questions carrying 3 marks each.
  5. Section - C Q. No. 12 is case based question carrying 5 marks.
  6. Use of log tables and calculators is NOT allowed.

SECTION - A
1.

Predict the reagent for carrying out the following transformations: (Any two)

  1. Benzoyl chloride to Benzaldehyde
  2. Ethanal to 3-hydroxy butanal
  3. Ethanoic acid to 2-chloroethanoic acid
 2
2.
  1. Why on dilution the {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} of CH3COOH increases very fast, while that of CH3COONa increases gradually?
  2. What happens if external potential applied becomes greater than {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} of electrochemical cell?
 2
3.

An Organic compound (A) with molecular formula C3H7NO on heating with Br2 and KOH forms a compound (B). Compound (B) on heating with CHCl3 and alcoholic KOH produces a foul smelling compound (C) and on reacting with C6H5SO2Cl forms a compound (D) which is soluble in alkali. Write the structures of (A), (B), (C) and (D).

 2

SECTION - B
4.

Account for the following:

  1. Cu2+ salts are coloured while Zn2+ salts are white.
  2. Eo value for the Mn3+/Mn2+ couple is much more positive than that for Cr3+/Cr2+.
  3. Transition metals form alloys.
 3
5.(i)

Calculate {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}{/tex} and log Kc for the following cell:
Ni(s) + 2Ag+(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Ni2+(aq) + 2Ag(s)
Given that {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = 1.05V, IF = 96,500 Cmol-1.

 3

OR
(ii)

Calculate the e.m.f. of the following cell at 298K:
Fe(s) | Fe2+ (0.001 M) || H+ (0.01 M) | H2(g) (1 bar) | Pt(s)
Given that {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = +0.44V
[log 2 = 0.3010 log 3 = 0.4771 log 10 = 1]

 3
6.
  1. Using valence bond theory, predict the hybridization and magnetic character of following:
    [CoF6]3- [Atomic number of Co = 27]
  2. Write IUPAC name of the following complex:
    [CoBr2(en)2]+
  3. How many ions are produced from the complex [Co(NH3)6]Cl2 in solution?
 3
7.(a)

Differentiate between the following:

  1. Adsorption and Absorption
  2. Lyophobic Sol and Lyophilic Sol
  3. Multimolecular Colloid and Macromolecular colloid.
 3

OR
(b)
  1. Define the following terms:
    1. Zeta Potential
    2. Coagulation
  2. Why a negatively charged sol is obtained when AgNO3 solution is added to KI solution?
 3
8.

Define transition metals. Why Zn, Cd and Hg are not called transition metals? How is the variability in oxidation states of transition metals different from that of p-block elements?

 3
9.(a)

What happens when

  1. Propanone is treated with CH3MgBr and then hydrolysed?
  2. Ethanal is treated with excess ethanol and acid?
  3. Methanal undergoes Cannizzaro reaction?
 3

OR
(b)

Write the main product in the following reactions:

  1. {tex}2 \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COCl}+\left(\mathrm{CH}_3\right)_2 \mathrm{Cd} \rightarrow{/tex}
  2. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{CHO}\xrightarrow{\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{Hg})/\mathrm{Conc}\;\mathrm{HCl}}{/tex}
 3
10.

Give reasons:

  1. Ammonolysis of alkyl halides is not a good method to prepare pure primary amines.
  2. Aniline does not give Friedel-Crafts reaction.
  3. Although -NH2 group is o/p directing in electrophilic substitution reactions, yet aniline on nitration gives good yield of m-nitroaniline.
 3
11.(a)
  1. Which acid of the following pair would you expect to be stronger?
    F-CH2-COOH or CH3-COOH
  2. Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of their boiling points:
    CH3CH2OH, CH3-CHO, CH3-COOH
  3. Give simple chemical test to distinguish between Benzaldehyde and Acetophenone.
 3

OR
(b)
  1. Which will undergo faster nucleophilic addition reaction?
    Acetaldehyde or Propanone
  2. What is the composition of Fehling's reagent?
  3. Draw structure of the semicarbazone of Ethanal.
 3

SECTION - C
12.

Read the following passage and answer the questions that follow:

The rate of reaction is concerned with decrease in concentration of reactants or increase in the concentration of products per unit time. It can be expressed as instantaneous rate at a particular instant of time and average rate over a large interval of time. A number of factors such as temperature, concentration of reactants, catalyst affect the rate of reaction. Mathematical representation of rate of a reaction is given by rate law:
Rate = k[A]x [B]y
x and y indicate how sensitive the rate is to the change in concentration of A and B. Sum of x + y gives the overall order of a reaction.
When a sequence of elementary reactions gives us the products, the reactions are called complex reactions. Molecularity and order of an elementary reaction are same. Zero order reactions are relatively uncommon but they occur under special conditions. All natural and artificial radioactive decay of unstable nuclei take place by first order kinetics.
(i)

What is the effect of temperature on the rate constant of a reaction?

 1
(ii)

For a reaction A + B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Product, the rate law is given by, Rate = k[A]2 [B]1/2. What is the order of the reaction?

 1
(iii)

How order and molecularity are different for complex reactions?

 1
(iv)

A first order reaction has a rate constant 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3s-1. How long will 6g of this reactant take to reduce to 2g?

 2

OR

The half life for radioactive decay of 14C is 6930 years. An archaeological artifact containing wood had only 75% of the 14C found in a living tree. Find the age of the sample.
[log 4 = 0.6021 log 3 = 0.4771 log 2 = 0.3010 log 10 = 1]

answer_key_html:
Annual Exam - 2022

Code: 56/3/1 (set-1)

  • Please check that this question paper contains 12 printed pages.
  • Q.P. Code number given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  • Please check that this question paper contains 12 questions.
  • Please write down the Serial Number of the question in the answer-book before attempting it.
  • 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the candidates will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.

CHEMISTRY (Theory)

Time allowed:2 hours
Maximum Marks:35

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This question paper contains 12 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper is divided into three Sections - Section A, B and C.
  3. Section - A Q. Nos. 1 to 3 are very short answer type questions carrying 2 marks each.
  4. Section - B Q. Nos. 4 to 11 are short answer type questions carrying 3 marks each.
  5. Section - C Q. No. 12 is case based question carrying 5 marks.
  6. Use of log tables and calculators is NOT allowed.

SECTION : A
1.

Predict the reagent for carrying out the following transformations: (Any two)

  1. Benzoyl chloride to Benzaldehyde
  2. Ethanal to 3-hydroxy butanal
  3. Ethanoic acid to 2-chloroethanoic acid
Solution:
  1. Rosenmund Reduction in presence of Lindlar's Catalyst (H2/Pd-BaSO4).
  2. Aldol Condensation in presence of dil. NaOH.
  3. Hell-Volhard Zelinsky Reaction in presence of Cl2/Red Phosphorous.
 2
2.
  1. Why on dilution the {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} of CH3COOH increases very fast, while that of CH3COONa increases gradually?
  2. What happens if external potential applied becomes greater than {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} of electrochemical cell?
Solution:
  1. In the case of CH3COOH, which is a weak electrolyte, the number of ions increase on dilution due to an increase in the degree of dissociation.
    {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} {/tex}{tex}\rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}(\mathrm{aq} .)+\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}{/tex}
    In the case of strong electrolyte such as CH3COONa, the number of ions remains the same but the interionic attraction between ions decreases on the dilution.
  2. The chemical reaction of the cell is reversed and current flows in the opposite direction when opposing emf is slightly greater than that of the cell.
 2
3.

An Organic compound (A) with molecular formula C3H7NO on heating with Br2 and KOH forms a compound (B). Compound (B) on heating with CHCl3 and alcoholic KOH produces a foul smelling compound (C) and on reacting with C6H5SO2Cl forms a compound (D) which is soluble in alkali. Write the structures of (A), (B), (C) and (D).

Solution:

{tex}CH_3\underset{(A)}{CH_2}CONH_2+Br_2+KOH\rightarrow CH\underset{(B)}{{}_3CH_2}NH_2{/tex}
{tex}CH_3\underset{(B)}{CH_2}NH_2+CHCl_3+KOH\rightarrow CH\underset{(C)}{{}_3CH_2}NC{/tex}

A = Propanaimde
B = Ethyl amine (1o amine)
C = Ethyl isocyanide
D = N-ethyl benzene sulphonamide

 2

SECTION : B
4.

Account for the following:

  1. Cu2+ salts are coloured while Zn2+ salts are white.
  2. Eo value for the Mn3+/Mn2+ couple is much more positive than that for Cr3+/Cr2+.
  3. Transition metals form alloys.
Solution:
  1. Zn2+ salts have a completely filled set of d-orbitals (3d10), while Cu2+ has an incompletely filled set of d-orbitals (3d9), and therefore, d-d transition is possible in Cu2+, leading to blue colour.
  2. In case of Mn '+2' oxidation state is more stable than '+3' oxidation state and in case of Cr '+3' oxidation state is more stable than '+2' oxidation state.
  3. Transition metals have very similar atomic sizes as a result one metal can easily replace the other metal from its lattice to form solid solution (alloy). Transition metals are also miscible with one another in the molten state. Thus, the molten state solution of two or more transition metals on cooling forms an alloy.
 3
5.(i)

Calculate {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}{/tex} and log Kc for the following cell:
Ni(s) + 2Ag+(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Ni2+(aq) + 2Ag(s)
Given that {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = 1.05V, IF = 96,500 Cmol-1.

Solution:

According to the equation,
Ni + 2Ag+ {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Ni2+ + 2Ag
{tex} \Delta{/tex}G = -nFEo
Where {tex} \Delta{/tex}G = Gibb's free energy
{tex} \Delta{/tex}G = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.05
N = No. of electrons gain or lost = 2
{tex} \Delta{/tex}G = -202.650 kJ
F = Faraday's constant = 96500
Eo = Standard emf = 1.05V
The relation between Gibb's free energy and Equilibrium constant is given by the equation
{tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.0591}{n} \log K_c{/tex}
log KC = {tex}-\frac{1.05 \times 2}{0.0591}{/tex} = 35.3
KC = 3.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 1035

 3

OR
(ii)

Calculate the e.m.f. of the following cell at 298K:
Fe(s) | Fe2+ (0.001 M) || H+ (0.01 M) | H2(g) (1 bar) | Pt(s)
Given that {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = +0.44V
[log 2 = 0.3010 log 3 = 0.4771 log 10 = 1]

Solution:

According to the equation,
Fe(s) + 2H+ (aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Fe2+ (aq) + H2(g)
{tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-E_{\text {anode }}^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0 - (-0.44) V
{tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = + 0.44V
By applying Nernst Equation
Ecell = {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{\left[F e^{2+}\right]}{\left[H^{+}\right]^2}{/tex}
Ecell = {tex}0.44 \frac{-0.0591}{2} \log \frac{0.001}{1^2}{/tex}
Ecell = {tex}0.44-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log 10^{-3}{/tex}
Ecell = 0.44 + 0.089V
Ecell = +0.53V

 3
6.
  1. Using valence bond theory, predict the hybridization and magnetic character of following:
    [CoF6]3- [Atomic number of Co = 27]
  2. Write IUPAC name of the following complex:
    [CoBr2(en)2]+
  3. How many ions are produced from the complex [Co(NH3)6]Cl2 in solution?
Solution:
  1. sp3d2, paramagnetic
  2. dibromidobis(ethane-1,2-diamine)cobalt (III) ion
  3. 3
 3
7.(a)

Differentiate between the following:

  1. Adsorption and Absorption
  2. Lyophobic Sol and Lyophilic Sol
  3. Multimolecular Colloid and Macromolecular colloid.
Solution:

Chapter Delete from the Textbook.

 3

OR
(b)
  1. Define the following terms:
    1. Zeta Potential
    2. Coagulation
  2. Why a negatively charged sol is obtained when AgNO3 solution is added to KI solution?
Solution:

Chapter Delete from the Textbook.

 3
8.

Define transition metals. Why Zn, Cd and Hg are not called transition metals? How is the variability in oxidation states of transition metals different from that of p-block elements?

Solution:

Metals which have incomplete d-orbital in ground state or in its oxidation state.
Because of completely filled d-orbitals in ground state or in its oxidation state.
Oxidation states differ by +1 unit in transition metals whereas by +2 units in p-block elements / heavier elements are stable in higher oxidation state in transition elements whereas heavier elements are stable in lower oxidation states in p-block elements.

 3
9.(a)

What happens when

  1. Propanone is treated with CH3MgBr and then hydrolysed?
  2. Ethanal is treated with excess ethanol and acid?
  3. Methanal undergoes Cannizzaro reaction?
Solution:
  1. When propanone is treated with methylmagnesium Bromide and then hydrolysed, tertiary butyl alcohol is formed.
    CH3 - CO - CH3 + CH3MgBr {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} (CH3)3C - OMgBr {tex}\xrightarrow{H_3O^+}{/tex} (CH3)3COH
  2. When ethanal reacts with excess ethanol and acid gives acetal.
  3. Methanal will undergo cannizzaro reaction as it possesses {tex}\alpha{/tex} - hydrogen to form methanol
 3

OR
(b)

Write the main product in the following reactions:

  1. {tex}2 \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COCl}+\left(\mathrm{CH}_3\right)_2 \mathrm{Cd} \rightarrow{/tex}
  2. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{CHO}\xrightarrow{\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{Hg})/\mathrm{Conc}\;\mathrm{HCl}}{/tex}
Solution:
  1. (Propanone)
  2. (Propane)
  3. (Cyclohexane)
 3
10.

Give reasons:

  1. Ammonolysis of alkyl halides is not a good method to prepare pure primary amines.
  2. Aniline does not give Friedel-Crafts reaction.
  3. Although -NH2 group is o/p directing in electrophilic substitution reactions, yet aniline on nitration gives good yield of m-nitroaniline.
Solution:
  1. Because it gives a mixture of amines which is difficult to separate.
  2. Aniline is a Lewis base and it reacts with AlCl3 to form a salt. 
  3. Because of protonation of aniline / formation of anilinium ion which deactivates the ring.
 3
11.(a)
  1. Which acid of the following pair would you expect to be stronger?
    F-CH2-COOH or CH3-COOH
  2. Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of their boiling points:
    CH3CH2OH, CH3-CHO, CH3-COOH
  3. Give simple chemical test to distinguish between Benzaldehyde and Acetophenone.
Solution:
  1. F- CH2COOH
  2. CH3CHO < CH3CH2OH < CH3COOH
  3. On warming with Tollens' reagent, benzaldehyde forms silver mirror whereas acetophenone does not.
 3

OR
(b)
  1. Which will undergo faster nucleophilic addition reaction?
    Acetaldehyde or Propanone
  2. What is the composition of Fehling's reagent?
  3. Draw structure of the semicarbazone of Ethanal.
Solution:
  1. Acetaldehyde
  2. Aqueous copper sulphate solution and alkaline solution of sodium-potassium tartarate (Rochelle's salt).
  3. CH3CH = NNHCONH2
 3

SECTION : C
12.

Read the following passage and answer the questions that follow:

The rate of reaction is concerned with decrease in concentration of reactants or increase in the concentration of products per unit time. It can be expressed as instantaneous rate at a particular instant of time and average rate over a large interval of time. A number of factors such as temperature, concentration of reactants, catalyst affect the rate of reaction. Mathematical representation of rate of a reaction is given by rate law:
Rate = k[A]x [B]y
x and y indicate how sensitive the rate is to the change in concentration of A and B. Sum of x + y gives the overall order of a reaction.
When a sequence of elementary reactions gives us the products, the reactions are called complex reactions. Molecularity and order of an elementary reaction are same. Zero order reactions are relatively uncommon but they occur under special conditions. All natural and artificial radioactive decay of unstable nuclei take place by first order kinetics.

 5
(i)

What is the effect of temperature on the rate constant of a reaction?

Solution:

The rate constant (k) for a reaction increases with an increase in temperature and becomes almost double with every 10o rises in temperature. This effect is expressed by Arrhenius equation:
k = {tex}\frac{A e^{-E a}}{R T}{/tex}

 1
(ii)

For a reaction A + B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Product, the rate law is given by, Rate = k[A]2 [B]1/2. What is the order of the reaction?

Solution:

According to the equation, r = k[A]2 [B]1/2
Order of the reaction = {tex}2+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{5}{2}{/tex}

 1
(iii)

How order and molecularity are different for complex reactions?

Solution:

Order of reaction is defined as the sum of the powers of the molar concentration of the reaction species in the rate equation of the reaction. It is applicable to both elementary and complex reactions.
Molecularity of a reaction is defined as the total number of reacting species participating in an elementary reaction. It has no significance for complex reactions as applicable only to elementary reactions.

 1
(iv)

A first order reaction has a rate constant 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3s-1. How long will 6g of this reactant take to reduce to 2g?

Solution:

k = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 s-1
t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_0}{R}{/tex}
t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \log \frac{6}{2}{/tex}
t = 1151.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.4771
= 549.3 s

 2

OR

The half life for radioactive decay of 14C is 6930 years. An archaeological artifact containing wood had only 75% of the 14C found in a living tree. Find the age of the sample.
[log 4 = 0.6021 log 3 = 0.4771 log 2 = 0.3010 log 10 = 1]

Solution:

According to first-order reaction,
Half-life (t1/2) = {tex}\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex}
t1/2 = 6980 years
k = 0.693/6980
However, the time taken can be calculated using the first-order rate of reaction when wood contains only 75% of C14.
Initial concentration of C14, [R]0 = 100
Amount at time t s, [R] = 75
t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_0}{[R]}{/tex}
t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{\frac{0.693}{6980}} \log \frac{100}{75}{/tex}
t = 2898 years
Thus, the age of the sample is approximately 2898 years.

 2

CHEMISTRY (Theory) Time allowed:2 hours Maximum Marks:35 General Instructions : Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them: This question paper contains 12 questions. All questions are compulsory. This question paper is divided into three Sections - Section A , B and C . Section - A Q. Nos. 1 to 3 are very short answer type questions carrying 2 marks each. Section - B Q. Nos. 4 to 11 are short answer type questions carrying 3 marks each. Section - C Q. No. 12 is case based question carrying 5 marks. Use of log tables and calculators is NOT allowed. SECTION : A 1. Predict the reagent for carrying out the following transformations: (Any two ) Benzoyl chloride to Benzaldehyde Ethanal to 3-hydroxy butanal Ethanoic acid to 2-chloroethanoic acid Solution: Rosenmund Reduction in presence of Lindlar's Catalyst (H 2 /Pd-BaSO 4 ). Aldol Condensation in presence of dil. NaOH. Hell-Volhard Zelinsky Reaction in presence of Cl 2 /Red Phosphorous. 2 2. Why on dilution the {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} of CH 3 COOH increases very fast, while that of CH 3 COONa increases gradually? What happens if external potential applied becomes greater than {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} of electrochemical cell? Solution: In the case of CH 3 COOH, which is a weak electrolyte, the number of ions increase on dilution due to an increase in the degree of dissociation. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} {/tex} {tex}\rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}(\mathrm{aq} .)+\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}{/tex} In the case of strong electrolyte such as CH 3 COONa, the number of ions remains the same but the interionic attraction between ions decreases on the dilution. The chemical reaction of the cell is reversed and current flows in the opposite direction when opposing emf is slightly greater than that of the cell. 2 3. An Organic compound (A) with molecular formula C 3 H 7 NO on heating with Br 2 and KOH forms a compound (B). Compound (B) on heating with CHCl 3 and alcoholic KOH produces a foul smelling compound (C) and on reacting with C 6 H 5 SO 2 Cl forms a compound (D) which is soluble in alkali. Write the structures of (A), (B), (C) and (D). Solution: {tex}CH_3\underset{(A)}{CH_2}CONH_2+Br_2+KOH\rightarrow CH\underset{(B)}{{}_3CH_2}NH_2{/tex} {tex}CH_3\underset{(B)}{CH_2}NH_2+CHCl_3+KOH\rightarrow CH\underset{(C)}{{}_3CH_2}NC{/tex} A = Propanaimde B = Ethyl amine (1 o amine) C = Ethyl isocyanide D = N-ethyl benzene sulphonamide 2 SECTION : B 4. Account for the following: Cu 2+ salts are coloured while Zn 2+ salts are white. E o value for the Mn 3+ /Mn 2+ couple is much more positive than that for Cr 3+ /Cr 2+ . Transition metals form alloys. Solution: Zn 2+ salts have a completely filled set of d-orbitals (3d 10 ), while Cu 2+ has an incompletely filled set of d-orbitals (3d 9 ), and therefore, d-d transition is possible in Cu 2+ , leading to blue colour. In case of Mn '+2' oxidation state is more stable than '+3' oxidation state and in case of Cr '+3' oxidation state is more stable than '+2' oxidation state. Transition metals have very similar atomic sizes as a result one metal can easily replace the other metal from its lattice to form solid solution (alloy). Transition metals are also miscible with one another in the molten state. Thus, the molten state solution of two or more transition metals on cooling forms an alloy. 3 5. (i) Calculate {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}{/tex} and log K c for the following cell: Ni(s) + 2Ag + (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Ni 2+ (aq) + 2Ag(s) Given that {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = 1.05V, IF = 96,500 Cmol -1 . Solution: According to the equation, Ni + 2Ag + {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Ni 2+ + 2Ag {tex} \Delta{/tex} G = -nFE o Where {tex} \Delta{/tex} G = Gibb's free energy {tex} \Delta{/tex} G = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.05 N = No. of electrons gain or lost = 2 {tex} \Delta{/tex} G = -202.650 kJ F = Faraday's constant = 96500 E o = Standard emf = 1.05V The relation between Gibb's free energy and Equilibrium constant is given by the equation {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.0591}{n} \log K_c{/tex} log K C = {tex}-\frac{1.05 \times 2}{0.0591}{/tex} = 35.3 K C = 3.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 35 3 OR (ii) Calculate the e.m.f. of the following cell at 298K: Fe(s) | Fe 2+ (0.001 M) || H + (0.01 M) | H 2 (g) (1 bar) | Pt(s) Given that {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = +0.44V [log 2 = 0.3010 log 3 = 0.4771 log 10 = 1] Solution: According to the equation, Fe(s) + 2H + (aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Fe 2+ (aq) + H 2 (g) {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-E_{\text {anode }}^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0 - (-0.44) V {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = + 0.44V By applying Nernst Equation E cell = {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{\left[F e^{2+}\right]}{\left[H^{+}\right]^2}{/tex} E cell = {tex}0.44 \frac{-0.0591}{2} \log \frac{0.001}{1^2}{/tex} E cell = {tex}0.44-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log 10^{-3}{/tex} E cell = 0.44 + 0.089V E cell = +0.53V 3 6. Solution: Chapter Delete from the Textbook. 3 8. Define transition metals. Why Zn, Cd and Hg are not called transition metals? How is the variability in oxidation states of transition metals different from that of p-block elements? Solution: Metals which have incomplete d-orbital in ground state or in its oxidation state. Because of completely filled d-orbitals in ground state or in its oxidation state. Oxidation states differ by +1 unit in transition metals whereas by +2 units in p-block elements / heavier elements are stable in higher oxidation state in transition elements whereas heavier elements are stable in lower oxidation states in p-block elements. 3 9. (a) What happens when Propanone is treated with CH 3 MgBr and then hydrolysed? Ethanal is treated with excess ethanol and acid? Methanal undergoes Cannizzaro reaction? Solution: When propanone is treated with methylmagnesium Bromide and then hydrolysed, tertiary butyl alcohol is formed. CH 3 - CO - CH 3 + CH 3 MgBr {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} (CH 3 ) 3 C - OMgBr {tex}\xrightarrow{H_3O^+}{/tex} (CH 3 ) 3 COH When ethanal reacts with excess ethanol and acid gives acetal. Methanal will undergo cannizzaro reaction as it possesses {tex}\alpha{/tex} - hydrogen to form methanol 3 OR (b) Write the main product in the following reactions: {tex}2 \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COCl}+\left(\mathrm{CH}_3\right)_2 \mathrm{Cd} \rightarrow{/tex} {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{CHO}\xrightarrow{\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{Hg})/\mathrm{Conc}\;\mathrm{HCl}}{/tex} Solution: (Propanone) (Propane) (Cyclohexane) 3 10. Give reasons: Ammonolysis of alkyl halides is not a good method to prepare pure primary amines. Aniline does not give Friedel-Crafts reaction. Although -NH 2 group is o/p directing in electrophilic substitution reactions, yet aniline on nitration gives good yield of m-nitroaniline. Solution: Because it gives a mixture of amines which is difficult to separate. Aniline is a Lewis base and it reacts with AlCl 3 to form a salt. Solution: Acetaldehyde Aqueous copper sulphate solution and alkaline solution of sodium-potassium tartarate (Rochelle's salt). CH 3 CH = NNHCONH 2 3 SECTION : C 12. Read the following passage and answer the questions that follow: The rate of reaction is concerned with decrease in concentration of reactants or increase in the concentration of products per unit time. It can be expressed as instantaneous rate at a particular instant of time and average rate over a large interval of time. A number of factors such as temperature, concentration of reactants, catalyst affect the rate of reaction. Mathematical representation of rate of a reaction is given by rate law: Rate = k[A] x [B] y x and y indicate how sensitive the rate is to the change in concentration of A and B. Sum of x + y gives the overall order of a reaction. When a sequence of elementary reactions gives us the products, the reactions are called complex reactions. Molecularity and order of an elementary reaction are same. Zero order reactions are relatively uncommon but they occur under special conditions. All natural and artificial radioactive decay of unstable nuclei take place by first order kinetics. 5 (i) What is the effect of temperature on the rate constant of a reaction? Solution: The rate constant (k) for a reaction increases with an increase in temperature and becomes almost double with every 10 o rises in temperature. This effect is expressed by Arrhenius equation: k = {tex}\frac{A e^{-E a}}{R T}{/tex} 1 (ii) For a reaction A + B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Product, the rate law is given by, Rate = k[A] 2 [B] 1/2 . What is the order of the reaction? Solution: According to the equation, r = k[A] 2 [B] 1/2 Order of the reaction = {tex}2+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{5}{2}{/tex} 1 (iii) How order and molecularity are different for complex reactions? Solution: Order of reaction is defined as the sum of the powers of the molar concentration of the reaction species in the rate equation of the reaction. It is applicable to both elementary and complex reactions. Molecularity of a reaction is defined as the total number of reacting species participating in an elementary reaction. It has no significance for complex reactions as applicable only to elementary reactions. 1 (iv) A first order reaction has a rate constant 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 s -1 . How long will 6g of this reactant take to reduce to 2g? Solution: k = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 s -1 t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_0}{R}{/tex} t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \log \frac{6}{2}{/tex} t = 1151.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.4771 = 549.3 s 2 OR The half life for radioactive decay of 14 C is 6930 years. An archaeological artifact containing wood had only 75% of the 14 C found in a living tree. Find the age of the sample. [log 4 = 0.6021 log 3 = 0.4771 log 2 = 0.3010 log 10 = 1] Solution: According to first-order reaction, Half-life (t 1/2 ) = {tex}\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex} t 1/2 = 6980 years k = 0.693/6980 However, the time taken can be calculated using the first-order rate of reaction when wood contains only 75% of C 14 . Initial concentration of C 14 , [R] 0 = 100 Amount at time t s, [R] = 75 t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_0}{[R]}{/tex} t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{\frac{0.693}{6980}} \log \frac{100}{75}{/tex} t = 2898 years Thus, the age of the sample is approximately 2898 years. 2
